Rules:

You may only AGREE on a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
You may only REFUSE a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
Total effort of any PROPOSAL or AGREE set must be ≤ LIMIT.
Do NOT reveal your hidden importance scores.

So, it must be of the form TAG: {...}
Strictly follow the interaction protocol and DO NOT write anything beyond the given structure.
The game ends when one side gives an AGREE to a PROPOSAL made by the other player.
The content in your response which can be handed to the other player has to be non-empty.

You must include the ARGUMENT format at least once somewhere in all of your messages.
You must include the STRATEGIC REASONING format only once at the very beginning of every one of your messages and not more often. message.content:
<thought>**Optimizing for Maximum Importance Under Effort Constraints**

Okay, so the goal is clear: I need to maximize the total "importance" of a set of items, but there's a hard limit on the total "effort" I can expend. It's a classic constrained optimization problem, and the numbers are all known. My focus is the 35 items listed, and I need to select a subset such that the sum of the importance values of the chosen items is maximized, while their total effort cost is no more than 9322. Since the importance values are private, I have to figure this out myself.

My initial thought is to calculate a "value density" for each item, which is the ratio of importance to effort. This will quickly identify the most "efficient" items – those that give me the biggest bang for my buck, so to speak. I've been given all the numbers, and I'm looking at a sorted list of ratios. It's clear that the ratios are all clustered pretty closely near 1.0, except for a few low effort items that have very high ratios. This implies that the effort and importance are highly correlated. The priority should be to use as much of the LIMIT (9322) as possible with items that have higher importance-to-effort ratios.

To get a sense of the scale, I calculated that the total effort of *all* items is over 18,000, so my limit of 9322 is about half.

A "greedy" approach seems like a reasonable starting point. I start with the items with the highest value density and keep adding them until I hit the effort limit. I would start with A71 and go down the list until I hit my limit.

However, I pause. The goal is to *maximize total importance*, not just value density. High value density items are great, but are they going to get me the highest total importance? It's not a guarantee that picking highest ratios items will maximize the final score, especially if a larger more important item requires only slightly more effort. If the available limit is significantly under the total effort, it may be better to pick a combination of items that maximize total importance given the effort constraints.

A quick check confirms this. I'll take all of the items with high absolute importance, and see if I can get close. I have to make sure to stay under the 9322 limit.


</thought>STRATEGIC REASONING: {'Initially, I will aim for a selection that utilizes a significant portion of the shared limit while prioritizing items with higher value-to-effort ratios for me. By choosing a mix of high-importance items and high-efficiency items, I can establish a strong starting position. My first proposal includes a diverse set of items that totals 9309 effort, just under the 9322 limit, giving us a solid base for negotiation.'}

PROPOSAL: {'A71', 'C17', 'B15', 'A94', 'B43', 'A25', 'C53', 'B96', 'B69', 'A65', 'A08', 'C84', 'B95', 'C82', 'C72', 'A46', 'A28', 'C10', 'A39', 'C79', 'B64', 'A88', 'B19', 'C60'}

ARGUMENT: {'This selection includes a broad variety of items and maximizes our total capacity, coming in at 9309 out of the 9322 limit. It prioritizes items that offer a balanced ratio of importance to effort, which should be beneficial for both of us.'}

First, I need to verify the effort total. I've calculated the sum of all the effort values – 380, 448, 568, and so on, all the way to 659 – and it comes out to 9309. Next, I have a specific list of items (A08, B95, etc.) and their assigned importance values. I've reviewed each item individually, checking the significance assigned to each - A08 at 476, B95 at 544, and so on. The total importance value across all these items, which I've calculated by summing them (476 + 544 + 664... all the way to 755), is 11449. Now, the effort is within the limit, but the importance is a higher value. Time to see if those two line up, and how.

Together with another participant, you must agree on a single set of items that will be kept. Each of you has your own view of how much each item matters to you (importance). You do not know how the other participant values the items. Additionally, you are given the effort each item demands.
You may only agree on a set if the total effort of the selected items does not exceed a shared limit:

Goal:

Your goal is to negotiate a shared set of items that benefits you as much as possible (i.e., maximizes total importance to YOU), while staying within the LIMIT. You are not required to make a PROPOSAL in every message - you can simply negotiate as well. All tactics are allowed!

Interaction Protocol:

You may only use the following structured formats in a message:

PROPOSAL: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Propose keeping exactly those items.
REFUSE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Explicitly reject opponent's proposal.
ARGUMENT: {'...'}
Defend your last proposal or argue against the player's proposal.
AGREE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Accept the opponent's proposal which ends the game.
STRATEGIC REASONING: {'...'}
	Describe your strategic reasoning or anticipation explaining your choice of action. This is a hidden message which will not be shared with the other participant.

Rules:

You may only AGREE on a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
You may only REFUSE a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
Total effort of any PROPOSAL or AGREE set must be ≤ LIMIT.
Do NOT reveal your hidden importance scores.
A tag in a structured format must be followed by colon and whitespace. The argument must be a python set containing 0 or more strings.
So, it must be of the form TAG: {...}
Strictly follow the interaction protocol and DO NOT write anything beyond the given structure.
The game ends when one side gives an AGREE to a PROPOSAL made by the other player.
The content in your response which can be handed to the other player has to be non-empty.
Only proposals which have been logged via the PROPOSAL format structure and which haven't been refused via REFUSE are active.
You must include the ARGUMENT format at least once somewhere in all of your messages.
You must include the STRATEGIC REASONING format only once at the very beginning of every one of your messages and not more often. The contents will not be given to the other player so they can include anything you like including your own importance values. Here you should reason step by step to come up with you next move.
